summary: Joel Spolsky reflects on his two self-funded sabbaticals and advocates for a pattern of working four years followed by a year off. He traces his career from Microsoft through consulting and startup attempts, describing how his first sabbatical in 1995 included failed startup planning, self-education about the Internet, and a cross-country bike trip that left him refreshed and eager to return to work. His second sabbatical, taken after burning out at Juno, convinced him that founding a startup was his next move. He argues that starting companies carries less risk than people assume, given the abundance of venture capital and reasonable odds of a liquidity event over a career.
key_points: A regular sabbatical cycle (four years working, one year off) prevents burnout and restores genuine excitement for work
Microsoft's hiring model of relocating recent graduates to Redmond created social isolation that kept employees working long hours by default
Startup ideas are worth little without execution and the right partner — Joel had specs for what could have been Yahoo-scale companies but couldn't build them alone
Starting a company is less risky than perceived: venture money is abundant, startups pay competitive salaries, and the odds of a liquidity event over multiple attempts are favorable
Bad management doesn't just slow companies down — it causes a steady drain of talented, frustrated people who eventually leave
money_quotes: But it's not the idea that matters, it's the execution — an idea I will return to many times in this weblog.
For the average geek, this formula means that you spend all your time at work.
A quick calculation of my stock options showed that I had accumulated about $120,000 in 2 1/2 years at 'the soft', and, by my calculations, I thought I could afford the risk of working at a startup.
Somewhere in Idaho, riding through an empty road, my mood changed; I felt totally rested and eager to get back to work.
I've gradually come to realize that there is nothing really risky about starting a company these days.
I joined the flow of talented, frustrated people streaming for the doors.
The intense politicalness and arrogance of management there convinced me that I had almost no chance of changing things.
key_insight: Regular sabbaticals restore the excitement and clarity needed to do your best work, and the financial risk of taking time off — or starting a company — is far lower than most people assume.
